{{Short description|Psychological effect on perceptions of attractiveness}}
[[File:Brown University Cheerleaders at Brown Stadium football game.jpg|thumb|[[Brown University]] cheerleaders]]
The '''cheerleader effect''', also known as the '''group attractiveness effect''' or the '''friend effect''',<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |last1=Ying |first1=Haojiang |last2=Burns |first2=Edwin |last3=Lin |first3=Xinyi |last4=Xu |first4=Hong |date=Mar 2019 |title=Ensemble statistics shape face adaptation and the cheerleader effect. |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: General |language=en |volume=148 |issue=3 |pages=421–436 |doi=10.1037/xge0000564 |pmid=30802125 |hdl=10356/145719 |s2cid=73460597 |issn=1939-2222|hdl-access=free }}</ref> is a proposed [[cognitive bias]] which causes people to perceive individuals as 1.5–2.0% more attractive in a group than when seen alone.<ref name=":1">{{Cite journal |last1=Carragher |first1=Daniel J. |last2=Thomas |first2=Nicole A. |last3=Gwinn |first3=O. Scott |last4=Nicholls |first4=Michael E. R. |date=2020-08-17 |title=The cheerleader effect is robust to experimental manipulations of presentation time |journal=Journal of Cognitive Psychology |volume=32 |issue=5–6 |pages=553–561 |doi=10.1080/20445911.2020.1776718 |hdl=1893/31328 |issn=2044-5911|hdl-access=free }}</ref> The first paper to report this effect was written by Drew Walker and Edward Vul, in 2013.<ref name="Walker2013"/>

[[Physical attractiveness]] implies individuals' preferences in a [[sexual selection]] based on the [[evolutionary psychology]]. In 1979, [[Donald Symons]] first proposed this evolutionary explanation, suggesting that the evolving physical attractiveness results from mate assessment favoring partners who exhibited signs of good health and fertility, including face averageness.<ref>{{Cite book |last=Symons |first=Donald |title=The evolution of human sexuality |date=1979 |publisher=Oxford University Press |isbn=0-19-987847-1 |location=New York |oclc=1162574887}}</ref> This preference was proved to be shared across cultures.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Rhodes |first1=Gillian |author-link=Gillian Rhodes |last2=Yoshikawa |first2=Sakiko |last3=Clark |first3=Alison |last4=Lee |first4=Kieran |last5=McKay |first5=Ryan |last6=Akamatsu |first6=Shigeru |date=May 2001 |title=Attractiveness of Facial Averageness and Symmetry in Non-Western Cultures: In Search of Biologically Based Standards of Beauty |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1068/p3123 |journal=Perception |language=en |volume=30 |issue=5 |pages=611–625 |doi=10.1068/p3123 |issn=0301-0066 |pmid=11430245 |s2cid=15333152|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Two parts constitute physical attractiveness, and most former studies investigated underlying mechanisms leading to cheerleader effect specifically in its subset, facial attractiveness.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1" /><ref name=":3" /> Nevertheless, a study has recognized this effect in another physical appearance indicator, human body perceptions.<ref name=":4">{{Cite journal |last1=Hsieh |first1=Jean YJ |last2=Gwinn |first2=O Scott |last3=Brooks |first3=Kevin R |last4=Stephen |first4=Ian D |last5=Carragher |first5=Daniel J |last6=Nicholls |first6=Michael ER |date=May 2021 |title=The "cheerleader effect" in facial and bodily attractiveness: A result of memory bias and not perceptual encoding |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1747021820976087 |journal=Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology |language=en |volume=74 |issue=5 |pages=972–980 |doi=10.1177/1747021820976087 |pmid=33174508 |s2cid=226303220 |issn=1747-0218|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

The [[effect size]] of the cheerleader effect is not modulated by the presentation time,<ref name=":1" /> the number of individuals surrounding the target,<ref name="Walker2013"/> spatial arrangement of the faces in the group.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Carragher |first1=Daniel J. |last2=Lawrence |first2=Blake J. |last3=Thomas |first3=Nicole A. |last4=Nicholls |first4=Michael E. R. |date=2018-02-07 |title=Visuospatial asymmetries do not modulate the cheerleader effect |journal=Scientific Reports |language=en |volume=8 |issue=1 |page=2548 |doi=10.1038/s41598-018-20784-5 |issn=2045-2322 |pmc=5803192 |pmid=29416057|bibcode=2018NatSR...8.2548C }}</ref> However, another study argued that the arrangement of faces in the group might influence this effect since people's central viewing tendency might affect observers to focus more on the perceived attractiveness of the middle face in the group.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Bindemann |first=Markus |date=2010-11-23 |title=Scene and screen center bias early eye movements in scene viewing |journal=Vision Research |series=Vision Research Reviews |language=en |volume=50 |issue=23 |pages=2577–2587 |doi=10.1016/j.visres.2010.08.016 |pmid=20732344 |s2cid=18230236 |issn=0042-6989|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Findings of this effect are interdisciplinary in applications. Based on them, mate choice,<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=McDowell |first1=Jackie |last2=Starratt |first2=Valerie G. |date=Sep 2019 |title=Experimental examination and extension of the cheerleader effect |journal=Personality and Individual Differences |language=en |volume=147 |pages=245–249 |doi=10.1016/j.paid.2019.05.004 |s2cid=164332202 |issn=0191-8869}}</ref> marketing,<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal |last1=Rao |first1=Vithala R. |last2=Russell |first2=Gary J. |last3=Bhargava |first3=Hemant |last4=Cooke |first4=Alan |last5=Derdenger |first5=Tim |last6=Kim |first6=Hwang |last7=Kumar |first7=Nanda |last8=Levin |first8=Irwin |last9=Ma |first9=Yu |last10=Mehta |first10=Nitin |last11=Pracejus |first11=John |last12=Venkatesh |first12=R. |date=Mar 2018 |title=Emerging Trends in Product Bundling: Investigating Consumer Choice and Firm Behavior |journal=Customer Needs and Solutions |language=en |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=107–120 |doi=10.1007/s40547-017-0075-x |s2cid=256393478 |issn=2196-2928}}</ref> and social media<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Messner |first1=Claude |last2=Carnelli |first2=Mattia |last3=Höhener |first3=Patrick Stefan |date=2021 |title=Change in Evaluation Mode Can Cause a Cheerleader Effect |journal=Frontiers in Psychology |volume=12 |article-number=607448 |doi=10.3389/fpsyg.2021.607448 |issn=1664-1078 |pmc=8107816 |pmid=33981266 |doi-access=free }}</ref> tactics are designed to increase the attractiveness of a target individual or item via the help of the group.

== Origin ==
The phrase was coined by the fictional character [[Barney Stinson]] ([[Neil Patrick Harris]]) in "[[Not a Father's Day]]", an episode of the television series ''[[How I Met Your Mother]]'', first aired on November 10, 2008. Barney points out to his friends a group of women that initially seem attractive, but who are all unattractive when examined individually. This point is made again by two other characters, [[Ted Mosby]] ([[Josh Radnor]]) and [[Robin Scherbatsky]] ([[Cobie Smulders]]), later in the episode, who note that some of Barney's friends also only seem attractive in a group. This occurrence may be explained by the processing of Barney's [[visual system]], in which his brain automatically calculated a combined beauty level of that group of ladies. This overall impression then impacts his assessment of the specific female within that group, leading him to believe she is similar to the previously established better average attractiveness.<ref name=":4" />

== Conditions for the effect to occur ==

* [[Bias]] in recalling. The cheerleader effect occurred only when participants were asked to rate the attractiveness after images were removed from their vision, suggesting that an initial perceptual encoding with the existence of photos while rated could not lead to the effect.<ref name=":4" />
* Contrast effect. It was found that the cheerleader effect occurred when the target face was the most attractive face compared to other members in the group but not when it was the least attractive so that the comparison among faces is required.<ref name=":0" />

== Studies and proposed explanations ==
===First study===
In 2013, the first research was reported by Drew Walker and Edward Vul.<ref name="Walker2013"/> Across five studies, participants rated the attractiveness of male and female faces when shown in a group photo, and an individual photo, with the order of the photographs randomised. The cheerleader effect was quantified as the difference between the attractiveness ratings assigned in the experimental condition (in a group photo) and the control (in an isolated image) condition. It was found that participants consistently rated the person as more appealing in the group photograph compared to the individual picture.

This effect occurs with male-only, female-only and mixed gender groups, and both small and large groups. In addition, the effect occurs to the same extent with various group sizes of four and 16 people. Participants in studies looked more at the attractive people than the unattractive people in the group.

Drew Walker and Edward Vul proposed that this effect arises due to the interplay of three [[Cognitive psychology|cognitive phenomena]]:<ref name="Walker2013"/>
#The human [[visual system]] takes "ensemble representations" of faces in a group. This explanation was backed up by Timothy F. Brady and George A. Alvarez's findings in 2011.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brady |first1=Timothy F. |last2=Alvarez |first2=George A. |date=Mar 2011 |title=Hierarchical Encoding in Visual Working Memory: Ensemble Statistics Bias Memory for Individual Items |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0956797610397956 |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=22 |issue=3 |pages=384–392 |doi=10.1177/0956797610397956 |pmid=21296808 |s2cid=18030342 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref> In the study, participants were displayed with 30 sets of circles, and circles of various sizes surrounded a tested circle. When asked to determine the tested circle's size, observers' memory of its size is biased by the mean size of all circles shown to them to estimate, showing that people do not encode visual images in memory independently.
#Perception of individuals is biased towards this average. People's visual systems [[subconscious]]ly and automatically calculate the average facial impression so that any extreme is ruled out.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Haberman |first1=Jason |last2=Whitney |first2=David |date=2009 |title=Seeing the mean: Ensemble coding for sets of faces. |journal=Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance |language=en |volume=35 |issue=3 |pages=718–734 |doi=10.1037/a0013899 |issn=1939-1277 |pmc=2696629 |pmid=19485687}}</ref>
#"Attractive faces are only average."<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Langlois |first1=Judith H. |last2=Roggman |first2=Lori A. |date=Mar 1990 |title=Attractive Faces Are Only Average |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9280.1990.tb00079.x |journal=Psychological Science |language=en |volume=1 |issue=2 |pages=115–121 |doi=10.1111/j.1467-9280.1990.tb00079.x |s2cid=18557871 |issn=0956-7976|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Results showed that composited faces were rated as more attractive and typical without extreme features. Humans develop this preference for "[[prototype]]" face from early life since they are easily identified, and individuals could extract social information from these most facelike stimuli to aid [[social interaction]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Leopold |first1=David A. |last2=Rhodes |first2=Gillian |author-link2=Gillian Rhodes |date=2010 |title=A comparative view of face perception. |journal=Journal of Comparative Psychology |language=en |volume=124 |issue=3 |pages=233–251 |doi=10.1037/a0019460 |issn=1939-2087 |pmc=2998394 |pmid=20695655}}</ref>
When all three of these phenomena are taken together, researchers proposed that the cheerleader effect results from a "hierarchical encoding" and that the hierarchical structure of visual [[working memory]] makes observers summarize the group into an ensemble average. Specifically, the individual faces will seem more attractive in a group, as they appear more similar to the average group face, which is more attractive than members' faces.<ref name="Walker2013"/>

=== Follow-up studies ===
However, this causation proposal of "hierarchical encoding" was doubted by Carragher et al. in 2019, who found that this effect also occurred in contexts that "were incompatible with hierarchical encoding."<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Carragher |first1=Daniel J. |last2=Thomas |first2=Nicole A. |last3=Gwinn |first3=O. Scott |last4=Nicholls |first4=Mike E. R. |date=2019-06-27 |title=Limited evidence of hierarchical encoding in the cheerleader effect |journal=Scientific Reports |language=en |volume=9 |issue=1 |page=9329 |doi=10.1038/s41598-019-45789-6 |issn=2045-2322 |pmc=6597562 |pmid=31249339|bibcode=2019NatSR...9.9329C }}</ref> They then proposed another explanation: "Social inference mechanism." It implies that the social context of being surrounded by friends may elicit observers' positive inferences on the target's trait, like "friendly or likable, which causes an increase to their perceived attractiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Brewer |first1=G. |last2=Archer |first2=J. |date=Mar 2007 |title=What do people infer from facial attractiveness? |url=https://akjournals.com/view/journals/1126/5/1/article-p39.xml |journal=Journal of Evolutionary Psychology |language=en-US |volume=5 |issue=1 |pages=39–49 |doi=10.1556/jep.2007.1002 |issn=1789-2082}}</ref> However, a recent study in 2021 tested this hypothesis in trustworthiness judgments. It is found that the trait inferences on one's facial trustworthiness did not experience the cheerleader effect.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Carragher |first1=Daniel J. |last2=Thomas |first2=Nicole A. |last3=Nicholls |first3=Michael E. R. |date=Nov 2021 |title=The dissociable influence of social context on judgements of facial attractiveness and trustworthiness |url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/bjop.12501 |journal=British Journal of Psychology |language=en |volume=112 |issue=4 |pages=902–933 |doi=10.1111/bjop.12501 |pmid=33742452 |s2cid=232296547 |issn=0007-1269|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

A 2015 study conducted by van Osch et al. confirmed the existence of the cheerleader effect obtained by Walker and Vul.<ref name=":8">{{Cite journal |last1=van Osch |first1=Yvette |last2=Blanken |first2=Irene |last3=Meijs |first3=Maartje H. J. |last4=van Wolferen |first4=Job |date=Apr 2015 |title=A Group's Physical Attractiveness Is Greater Than the Average Attractiveness of Its Members: The Group Attractiveness Effect |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/0146167215572799 |journal=Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin |language=en |volume=41 |issue=4 |pages=559–574 |doi=10.1177/0146167215572799 |pmid=25733515 |s2cid=35619191 |issn=0146-1672|hdl=10411/20549 |hdl-access=free |url-access=subscription }}</ref> Based on the effect, the research team offered two other potential explanations for it:<ref name=":8" />
*[[Selective attention]] to attractive group members. "People selectively attending to" and have longer fixation time on the most appealing members within a group so that they tend to make group rating based on "an average of the ratings of the most attractive group members"instead of taking every member's attractiveness within the group into account.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Leder |first1=Helmut |last2=Tinio |first2=Pablo P. L. |last3=Fuchs |first3=Isabella M. |last4=Bohrn |first4=Isabel |date=Sep 2010 |title=When attractiveness demands longer looks: The effects of situation and gender |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1080/17470211003605142 |journal=Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology |language=en |volume=63 |issue=9 |pages=1858–1871 |doi=10.1080/17470211003605142 |pmid=20373226 |s2cid=17748866 |issn=1747-0218|url-access=subscription }}</ref>
*The [[Gestalt psychology|Gestalt]] principle of similarity. It suggests that an initial perception of people's attractiveness is towards a group with a similar attractiveness degree as a whole, followed by the perception of its individual member's.
They claim that selective attention fits with the gathered data better.<ref name=":8" /> The explanation based on the Gestalt psychology was objected to in this study since researchers found that the effect only occur in the group with large variation in attractiveness. This finding thus was inconsistent with this principle of perceiving similar attractive people as a group to evaluate.

===Replication failure===
A 2015 replication of Walker and Vul's study failed to show any significant results for the group attractiveness effect. The research team hypothesized potential reasons for this. Firstly, this may be due to cultural differences, since the replication study was performed in Japan.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Ojiro |first1=Yuko |last2=Gobara |first2=Akihiko |last3=Nam |first3=Giyeon |last4=Sasaki |first4=Kyoshiro |last5=Kishimoto |first5=Reiki |last6=Yamada |first6=Yuki |last7=Miura |first7=Kayo |date=Jun 2015 |title=Two replications of "Hierarchical encoding makes individuals in a group seem more attractive (2014; Experiment 4)". |url=http://www.tqmp.org/ReplicationStudies/vol11-2/r008 |journal=The Quantitative Methods for Psychology |volume=11 |issue=2 |pages=r8–r11 |doi=10.20982/tqmp.11.2.r008 |issn=2292-1354|doi-access=free }}</ref> Secondly, the effect size was affected by the variation in the composition of members in a group. Researchers suggested that the cheerleader effect was less likely to occur for people with the similar attractiveness level in a group since the selection attention would not happen to bias participants' memory towards a higher attractiveness average.<ref name=":8" />

== Applications ==

* [[Marketing strategy]]. This effect was also found in non-human group images<ref name=":6" /> so its application in [[Consumer behaviour|consumer behaviors]] was investigated. For example, many firms employ [[product bundling]] by utilizing customers' psychology of integrating assessments of individual products within a package to create an overall evaluation of the entire package to strengthen the competitiveness of their target products in the market.<ref name=":5" />
* Dating strategy. Having friends to accompany with or displaying profile photos in a crowd, particularly "being surrounded by unattractive friends may help" to improve perceived attractiveness due to this effect.<ref name=":0" />

== Criticisms and prospects ==
It is argued that the perception of facial attractiveness may be influenced by the race information in the [[Stimulus (physiology)|stimulus]] face.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Rhodes |first1=Gillian |author-link=Gillian Rhodes |last2=Lee |first2=Kieran |last3=Palermo |first3=Romina |last4=Weiss |first4=Mahi |last5=Yoshikawa |first5=Sakiko |last6=Clissa |first6=Peter |last7=Williams |first7=Tamsyn |last8=Peters |first8=Marianne |last9=Winkler |first9=Chris |last10=Jeffery |first10=Linda |date=Mar 2005 |title=Attractiveness of Own-Race, Other-Race, and Mixed-Race Faces |url=http://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1068/p5191 |journal=Perception |language=en |volume=34 |issue=3 |pages=319–340 |doi=10.1068/p5191 |issn=0301-0066 |pmid=15895630 |s2cid=6199926|url-access=subscription }}</ref> The future study could display participants with diverse races of faces like mixed-raced composites to test this race effect.

In addition, repeated exposure to moderately attractive faces is found to reward the emotional system, and it is positively correlated to the perceived attractiveness.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Han |first1=Shangfeng |last2=Liu |first2=Shen |last3=Gan |first3=Yetong |last4=Xu |first4=Qiang |last5=Xu |first5=Pengfei |last6=Luo |first6=Yuejia |last7=Zhang |first7=Lin |date=Mar 2020 |title=Repeated exposure makes attractive faces more attractive: Neural responses in facial attractiveness judgement |journal=Neuropsychologia |language=en |volume=139 |article-number=107365 |doi=10.1016/j.neuropsychologia.2020.107365 |pmid=32001231 |s2cid=210928782 |issn=0028-3932}}</ref> Therefore, watching the target faces twice in a repeated measures design may contribute to observers' ratings of better attractiveness, regardless of the contribution of the cheerleader effect.

== References ==
{{Reflist|refs=

<ref name="Ojiro2015">{{cite journal|last1=Ojiro |first1=Yuko|last2=Gobara|first2=Akihiko|last3=Nam |first3=Giyeon|last4=Sasaki |first4=Kyoshiro |last5=Kishimoto|first5=Reiki|last6=Yamada|first6=Yuki |last7=Miura|first7=Kayo|title=Two replications of "Hierarchical encoding makes individuals in a group seem more attractive (2014; Experiment 4)"|journal=The Quantitative Methods for Psychology|date=2015|volume=11 |issue=2|pages=r8–r11|issn=2292-1354 |url=https://www.researchgate.net/publication/280492593 |doi=10.20982/tqmp.11.2.r008|doi-access=free}}</ref>

<ref name="Walker2013">{{Cite journal |title=Hierarchical Encoding Makes Individuals in a Group Seem More Attractive |url= http://www.evullab.org/pdf/WalkerVul-PS-2013.pdf |journal=Psychological Science |volume=25 |issue=1 |date=Jan 2013 |issn=0956-7976 |pmid=24163333 |pages=230–235 |doi=10.1177/0956797613497969 |first1=Drew |last1=Walker |first2=Edward |last2=Vul |s2cid= 16309135 |url-status=live |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20160304043410/http://www.evullab.org/pdf/WalkerVul-PS-2013.pdf|archive-date=2016-03-04}}</ref>

}}
